element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player learns to bounce ball off walls and destroy tiles in confined 3D space
Core puzzle loop: destroy all tiles on a wall to unlock passage to next room
Learning to identify and target power-ups strategically
Resource management: 40 starting bullets, max 99, required for efficient progression
Threat management: losing 5 balls costs 1 life; game over when all lives lost
Combat introduced as secondary concern to puzzle-solving
Map-making becomes critical; World 2 has 60+ rooms, establishing exploration as core mechanic

element_introduction_order.element: Advanced power-ups (ROW, SUPER BALL, TRIPLE, PINBALL)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Tempo-changing mechanics that allow room-clearing shortcuts and tactical advantages

element_introduction_order.element: Difficult tile patterns and enemy types
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Significantly harder hit zones; some enemies one-shot kill; increased precision required
Disorientation mechanic; map-making becomes essential survival strategy, not optional
Enemies tougher; more ways to lose balls; tighter hit detection on certain tiles

typical_relief_triggers: Clearing all tiles on a wall (passage unlocked, forward progress)
Obtaining ROW power-up (mass tile destruction, rapid room escape)
Finding extra life bonus (safety margin increased)
Reaching a new room via arrow navigation (successful maze progression)
typical_tension_triggers: Ball approaching danger zone (behind glove position)
Encountering one-shot enemy encounters in World 2-3
Running low on Robo-Bullets in tile-dense rooms
Random warp to unknown room (disorientation, map invalidation)
Difficult tile patterns with narrow hit zones
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Cyclical puzzle-exploration rhythm: calm tile-destruction phase interrupted by chaotic enemy encounters and navigation anxiety; tension builds through maze depth and world progression; relief comes from successful room transitions and power-up acquisition

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Question mark tiles within destructible wall surfaces indicate hidden power-ups; ROW and PINBALL power-ups reveal optional pathways
spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Arrow indicators point to newly unlocked passages after wall clearing; hidden level select via controller input sequence

difficulty_curve.early_game: Tutorial phase: basic ball-bouncing mechanics in confined 3D rooms; safe introduction to tile destruction and room navigation; minimal enemy presence
difficulty_curve.mid_game: World 1-2 transition: maze complexity increases dramatically (60+ rooms in World 2 alone); map-making becomes critical survival skill; power-up variety expands; first serious enemy encounters
difficulty_curve.late_game: World 3: enemy difficulty spikes significantly (one-shot kills); tile patterns become harder to hit; random warping increases disorientation; resource management (Robo-Bullets) becomes critical
notable_spikes: World 2 entry: massive room count jump and introduction of harder enemy types
World 2-3 transition: one-shot kill enemies introduced
Random warp mechanic: disruptive difficulty spike that punishes map-based progression strategy

notable_level_patterns.description: Single-room enclosed space with walls covered in destructible tiles (5-40 tiles per wall depending on wall position). Player bounces ball or shoots Robo-Bullets to destroy tiles until passage arrow appears. Difficulty increases with tile count and hit-zone precision requirements.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: World 1 early rooms, World 2 standard rooms

notable_level_patterns.description: Interconnected dungeon-like structure where clearing one room's walls opens passages to adjacent rooms. Requires spatial mapping and systematic navigation. World 2 has 60+ interconnected rooms, establishing exploration as dominant mechanic.
Rooms where enemy presence forces combat or evasion before tile-clearing can occur. World 2-3 enemies deliver one-shot kills, creating high-tension combat phases between puzzle-solving.

Rooms designed to encourage players to hunt question mark tiles for beneficial effects (ROW for speed-clearing, PINBALL for path revelation, SUPER BALL for ball speed control). Reward structure incentivizes exploration and risk-taking.

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Super Glove Ball article)
